words: This third conversation with Eugene Setttle takes place underneath a big willow
tree in the backyard at his home in Moscow, Idaho on August 4, 1975. The inter
viewer is Sam Schrager.
SAM SCHRAGER: Used to be all those orchards around here.
EUGENE SETTLE: Yeah,we used to have lots of orchards out here. Now, you take
out here on Orchard Avenue, that runs out through there now; clear out
there for two miles out there, and that was nothing but all
orchards. And our place was just over from Orchard Avenue. We owned a
place, that's where I grew up. And that was about 1912. I don't remem
ber when they started— it must have been right after World War I was
when I think they started it. Early 20's. We used to have a vinegar
plant here on North Main Street in the early days. In the fall of the
year they hauled the apples in to this vinegar plant. They didn't sell
'em on the market, they just hauled 'em in, wagon loads of 'em, haul 'em
in to the vinegar plant here. Seems like the plant burned down, I'm not
sure about that. Got fire, and I don't think they never did repair it.
It was right in there where the tractor company is there now. Right in
there.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know where they sent that vinegar?

speaker: ES
words: No, I don't know where they did. I imagine they shipped it all over. I

speaker: ES
words: don't know. I don't know whether it was like the pea— you see Moscow

speaker: ES
words: is supposed to be the dry pea capital of the world. And somebody was

speaker: ES
words: over in, I don't know whether it was Sweden or Norway and they bought some

speaker: ES
words: split peas and they was packaged here in Moscow. Then we used to have

speaker: ES
words: a flour mill down here. That had quite a wide range flour. I know

speaker: ES
words: the fellows talk about buy* Blue Stem flour— the flour they made was

speaker: ES
words: Blue Stem, they called it— and a fellow said that he'd been up in

speaker: ES
words: Montana, and said you could buy it up there just as cheap up there as

speaker: ES
words: you could right here at the mill. So since I put in about twenty years

speaker: ES
words: in the grain business I found out why they market like wheat—

speaker: ES
words: if you go down to one of these grain elevators and sell a load of wheat

speaker: ES
words: and then you turn right around and buy two bushels of it back, to this

speaker: ES
words: cost, what you paid for it, the freight to the coast is added to the price

speaker: ES
words: of it. If you buy it back, why they add the freight to the coast, it's

speaker: ES
words: just up that much. And so that's the way it is. Well, the same way with—

speaker: ES
words: When I was down in Virginia, I guess it was in '62, I went to a lumberyard

speaker: ES
words: down there, my brother he was doing some building, and this knotty pine

speaker: ES
words: lumber was cheaper down there, I could buy it cheaper down there than I

speaker: ES
words: could right here in Moscow.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that the shipping trouble was a big problem? To get it to

speaker: SS
words: markets from here?

speaker: ES
words: Well, it cost 'em something to ship it down there and that was added to

speaker: ES
words: it. But I think in that Enough, I believe that it was just the dealer

speaker: ES
words: margin was higher here, and you just get a bigger rakeoff here than down

speaker: ES
words: there.

speaker: SS
words: Was it Western white pine?

speaker: ES
words: Yeah. It may have come from Lewiston That's why  priced it. Western

speaker: ES
words: white pine on the board there, and I asked 'em where it was from and he

speaker: ES
words: said it was shipped from Lewiston. And I asked 'em what the price of it

speaker: ES
words: was and come back home and priced it here, and it was cheaper— it was

speaker: ES
words: a little cheaper than it was here.

speaker: SS
words: Do you remember where the markets were when your father was farming?

speaker: SS
words: Where they were shipping their grain to?

speaker: ES
words: I don't know where they were. But I imagine it was pretty much like it

speaker: ES
words: is now, because I know that when I was a little kid, they used to claim

speaker: ES
words: that Russia controlled the world wheat market.

speaker: ES
words: russia had a big crop our prices were down in the States because we was

speaker: ES
words: competing with Russia. I guess it was the low cost of labor and one thing

speaker: ES
words: and another, like it is now. But I heard that, if they had a big crop

speaker: ES
words: in Russia, why to look out for us.

speaker: SS
words: By the way, have you heard anything from your daughter about that old

speaker: SS
words: records that you made out?

speaker: ES
words: No, I haven't. I wrote to her about it. We bought a car for our grand

speaker: ES
words: daughter and they was supposed to come after it around the first of this

speaker: ES
words: month, and I wrote and told 'em to bring it with her if she found that,

speaker: ES
words: but she hasn't showed up yet.

speaker: SS
words: Could you tell me any more on your grandfather, because we didn't talk

speaker: SS
words: about him hardly, because of that. And if you don't get that soon, I'd

speaker: SS
words: really like to know something about— I know you didn't know him.

speaker: ES
words: No. I never knew my grandfather on my dad's side at all. And I don't

speaker: ES
words: remember my grandmother either. She was still alive when I was a baby

speaker: ES
words: but I don't remember her. I talked to my sister down there in Portland

speaker: ES
words: here about a month ago and she said she remembers her grandmother real

speaker: ES
words: well. I don't know whether she remembers my grandfatherj mY grand

speaker: ES
words: father on my father's side or not. Of course, we left my grandfather's

speaker: ES
words: to coiae out here,

speaker: ES
words: and grandmother's place , when we left stopped at my

speaker: ES
words: mother's folks and waited for the arrival of one of my brothers before

speaker: ES
words: we came West. My father's people, I don't remember— I remember one

speaker: ES
words: of his sisters— we met his sisters when we were on our way out here,

speaker: ES
words: one of his sisters. And then I met one of his cousins, first cousins—

speaker: ES
words: two of his cousins down in Los Angeles, I was down there in '52, I be

speaker: ES
words: lieve it was; visiting

speaker: SS
words: Do you know about how old he would have been when he got freed from

speaker: SS
words: slavery?

speaker: ES
words: How old he would have been?

speaker: ES
words: Yeah.

speaker: ES
words: No, I don't have no idea how old he would have been when he was freed,

speaker: ES
words: I don't know. I am sure he was just a young man. I presume in his

speaker: ES
words: early twenties, I don't know about that.

speaker: ES
words: Do you think he stayed

speaker: ES
words: I don't think my dad— I never did hear him say how old his father was

speaker: ES
words: twenty when he was born.

speaker: ES
words: Do you think he stayed in the same area, or did he go someplace else

speaker: ES
words: then?

speaker: ES
words: Well, after the Civil War ended, he was in North Carolina*and after the

speaker: ES
words: war ended, he got a job in a little one-horse sawmill in the hills of

speaker: ES
words: Grth Carolina. I don't know what place it was, but that was his first

speaker: ES
words: job after he was a free man. He went to work in this sawmill in North

speaker: ES
words: Carolina. And my grandmother, she never was a slave because she was

speaker: ES
words: Indian. But she was kind of a little maid there for thfefsawmill owner,

speaker: ES
words: ed and she was kind of a mistress

speaker: ES
words: just help around the housejv took care of the kids, to look after

speaker: ES
words: the kids around there, I don't know how long she had been with them.

speaker: ES
words: But, anyway, that was her job, she lived there with them and took care

speaker: ES
words: of the kids.

speaker: ES
words: So that's where they met?

speaker: ES
words: Uh-huh. And then they married, and it seems they left there right away

speaker: ES
words: and went to Mississippi. I don't know what the attraction was to Mis

speaker: ES
words: sissippi to take 'em from North Carolina, but anyway, they went to Mis

speaker: ES
words: sissippi. And my mother lived not too far from Corinth, Mississippi,

speaker: ES
words: and they wasn't too far apart. And there they raised— let's see I think

speaker: ES
words: there was— I think there was about three boys and four or five girls

speaker: ES
words: in my father's family. And I know that he had one brother that was

speaker: ES
words: older than he, and I don't know how many sisters. Anyway, they was liv

speaker: ES
words: ing in Mississippi. The family grew up there and raised their family

speaker: ES
words: in Mississippi. And then after they had raised a big family there,

speaker: ES
words: that's when my grandmother, they was pretty old people, they must have

speaker: ES
words: been old people, then when she put in for her allotment in the Indian

speaker: ES
words: territory. And that was just about the time when my dad and mother

speaker: ES
words: married, a little before that, I guess. When my grandfather passed

speaker: ES
words: away out there, we went out there, my dad and mother, they went out

speaker: ES
words: there to live with my grandmother to take care of the place. And my

speaker: ES
words: sister was, I think she was pretty near two years old when they went

speaker: ES
words: out there, because I was born after they went out there.

speaker: SS
words: You were born in Oklahoma Territory?

speaker: ES
words: Yeah. I was born in the Territory.

speaker: SS
words: You think that your father went out maybe because your grandfather had

speaker: SS
words: died, to take care of your grandmother?

speaker: ES
words: Yeah. That's why he went out there. He went out on this place there.

speaker: ES
words: My sister was talking about it when was down there— He worked

speaker: ES
words: there, you see all the good land was taken by that time, because it

speaker: ES
words: was throwed open I think in 1868, I think. So about all the good land

speaker: ES
words: was taken, course there was oil on the place^ but that wasn t even dis

speaker: ES
words: covered until about 19 must have been about '17 or '18 or maybe lat

speaker: ES
words: er than that. Anyway, my dad stayed there about a year or better when

speaker: ES
words: my grandmother died. He left the place— he'd left it before my grand

speaker: ES
words: mother died. Just a rock pile, he called it, he couldn't make a living

speaker: ES
words: on He had a good team and wagon, so he went to Arkansas; Fort Smith,

speaker: ES
words: Arkansas. Then my grandmother passed away and we went out there. And

speaker: ES
words: then from there— that's what I told you about— he heard about the great

speaker: ES
words: Northwest and he went West from there.

speaker: SS
words: You were telling me— or mentioned that you did a lot of hunting in those

speaker: SS
words: days.

speaker: ES
words: Yes, he was quite a hunter. He had lots of hound dogs. I remember

speaker: ES
words: when we was in Arkansas there, we lived — he farmed along the Arkansas

speaker: ES
words: River, and he had I don't know how many dogs, he had'^a bunch of hounds

speaker: ES
words: and he hunted a lot. And then people'd come out from Fort Smith, bus

speaker: ES
words: inessmen and their wives would come out and pay him so much to take him

speaker: ES
words: his old dogs to go possum hunting along the Arkansas River botton down

speaker: ES
words: there. And every so often a bunch would come out and go hunting at night.

speaker: ES
words: He was quite a hunter. And that's one thing that excited him about com

speaker: ES
words: ing West,  He thought that there was lots of game out here.

speaker: SS
words: Did you go hunting with him very much when you were growing up here?

speaker: ES
words: Oh, yeah, we hunted he^e lots. He brought hounds out here with him.

speaker: ES
words: all over Paradise Hill and back in the mountains here.

speaker: ES
words: We used to hunt coyotes^ We did lots of hunting after we come West,

speaker: ES
words: that is for coyotes. I don't believe he ever did go deer or elk hunting,

speaker: ES
words: he never did, I ton't think. He used to hunt coyotes.

speaker: SS
words: That sounds like that would be pretty hard kind of hunting to me. Those

speaker: SS
words: coyotes are pretty wily animals.

speaker: ES
words: Yeah they are. But we'd get after 'em with dogs, and the idea was,

speaker: ES
words: after the dogs'd get after one, you get out on some point or another

speaker: ES
words: and wait for 'em to bring 'em around in gunshot reach. Sometimes they'd

speaker: ES
words: take 'em clear out of the country, sometimes the coyote'd run in a cir

speaker: ES
words: cle, in different ways, you know. I know, my dad he hunted with

speaker: ES
words: his dogs he never had nothing but a shotgun and number six shot, so you

speaker: ES
words: know you gotta get pretty close to kill with number six shot.

speaker: ES
words: But he just kinda learned how to A lot of Moscow guys used to get

speaker: ES
words: my dad to go out hunting, and they used to come out on Saturdays in the

speaker: ES
words: wintertime and chase coyotes. It was quite a sport then. Then different

speaker: ES
words: ones around herer v got different dogs— I don't know whether you

speaker: ES
words: heard of the Weekses or not, they were Southerners and they had hounds

speaker: ES
words: and they did lots of hunting. Henry Weeks, I don't know whether he's

speaker: ES
words: the last of that generation, he had hounds, I don't know whether he's

speaker: ES
words: still got anyi or not. I haven't seen him for a long time. He always

speaker: ES
words: had dogs.

speaker: SS
words: When you would go hunting, would you go for a day, or would you camp

speaker: SS
words: out?

speaker: ES
words: No, just go for a day. 'Course I was about the only member of our family

speaker: ES
words: that went deer and elk hunting. I used to do that and go up in the Selway

speaker: ES
words: on the Lochsa River. We 'go up there in the fall of the year and

speaker: ES
words: camp out for a week at a time, there in the woods, hunt elk and deer.

speaker: ES
words: The last elk and deer I hunted— well, I've hunted 'em just around here

speaker: ES
words: quite a little bit. You take Joel out there, south of Joel , south

speaker: ES
words: and west of Joel back up in there. Thats pretty good— thdse whitetail

speaker: ES
words: deer, they're kind of a— they're not a domesticated deer, but you might

speaker: ES
words: find a whitetail deer out here in some farmer's field, up along the

speaker: ES
words: creek where there's brush, you know. They're kind of an open country

speaker: ES
words: animal, you're liable to catch^any place. They have come right downtown

speaker: ES
words: in Moscow here. You can see their prints in that store right across—

speaker: ES
words: down Washington Street on the building, right on the corner of Washing

speaker: ES
words: ton — Third and Washington, right across from the post office there.The store,

speaker: ES
words: Obergs used to own it there. One come down and got on Washington Street

speaker: ES
words: there and they tried to hem it up there and it tried to climb^that build

speaker: ES
words: ing, and it's hofif marks are in that— on the corner of that building

speaker: ES
words: there, where that deer tried to get away. Yeah, there have been several

speaker: ES
words: right here in Moscow. About two yeacs ago there was a deer right out

speaker: ES
words: here south of town out there by that implement snowmobile dealers out

speaker: ES
words: there. And then come out there and went out there towards the cemetery

speaker: ES
words: just between town and the cemetery there. A fellow tried to shoot it

speaker: ES
words: right out there by the fairgrounds. I guess that was the winter we had--

speaker: ES
words: in '70, I believe, '72— '71 or '72. '71, I guess that was the first

speaker: ES
words: winter had lost my legs.

speaker: SS
words: Did you do much with your brothers when you were growing up?

speaker: ES
words: Oh, yeah. We did lots of hunting, chased around. You take it on week

speaker: ES
words: ends, we was all in school the four of us, on Saturdays why we'd all

speaker: ES
words: get our guns we was quite outdoorsmen, all of us. We liked

speaker: ES
words: the outdoors, liked the hills. We did lots of hunting. We didn't hang

speaker: ES
words: around town too much, til we was grown. We just liked" outdoors

speaker: ES
words: and we all had horses and travel around.

speaker: SS
words: I wanted to ask you about the sports that they did in school

speaker: ES
words: Well, there was only two of us boys, myself and my second brother we

speaker: ES
words: were the only ones that you v ttok part in sports in school. When

speaker: ES
words: I was in high school I was on the track team and I played some football,

speaker: ES
words: but of course, I was awful small, I was about fourth stringer, I didn't

speaker: ES
words: play very much, but I played some football. And then my brother next

speaker: ES
words: to me— my second brother— he was on the Moscow High School track team.

speaker: ES
words: And then I had a nephew that was on the high school football team when

speaker: ES
words: they won the state champions. And then he played freshman , football

speaker: ES
words: up here at t the university and then he got hurt and then he didn't

speaker: ES
words: do it anymore. My younger brother, and the one next to me, they never

speaker: ES
words: took no part in any sports in high school or otherwise.

speaker: SS
words: Was track your favorite?

speaker: ES
words: Yes, it was.

speaker: SS
words: What was it like— sports those days when you were going to school?

speaker: SS
words: I mean like for track? Did you guys have uniforms and go around and

speaker: SS
words: play the schools?

speaker: ES
words: Yeah. Uh-huh. When I was in high school— the Moscow High School was

speaker: ES
words: track

speaker: ES
words: just beginning to come to it^s own in M because up until 1912- til

speaker: ES
words: they built the Whitworth— on the same side as the Methodist Church

speaker: ES
words: there— that was built in 1912 and I started there. And up until that

speaker: ES
words: time there was one building there that had the junior high and high

speaker: ES
words: school and I don't know how many smaller grades, was in the same buildall

speaker: ES
words: ing, see, because practically,the kids in those days, up until 1912

speaker: ES
words: they had a Prep at the University and they all went up there, you see.

speaker: ES
words: So the high school didn't even amount to much until they built the new

speaker: ES
words: high school. And they built the new high school I then they cut out

speaker: ES
words: the Prep department at the University there, and so the kids they had

speaker: ES
words: to go to high school^ but before that, when they had the Prep depart

speaker: ES
words: ment at the University there, when they finished the eighth grade why you'd

speaker: ES
words: go there and you'djtake you''four years of high school and then go right

speaker: ES
words: on through college, right there on the hill. But when they built this

speaker: ES
words: That's when I came in.

speaker: ES
words: new high school, then they cut the Prep department out. ^And so they

speaker: ES
words: ing just started an anthletic program when I started, just beginjto take

speaker: ES
words: hold. I know in football, why, we had to furnish our own uniforms.

speaker: ES
words: And then, of course, after that, athletic began to grow in then,of course,they

speaker: ES
words: of course it's a big deal now. They have everything, they

speaker: ES
words: have football and track and wrestling, tennis, lots of sports, the whole

speaker: ES
words: category of sports.

speaker: SS
words: What did you compete in?

speaker: ES
words: I was a distance runner. I held the high school record for the mile,

speaker: ES
words: for I don't know how many years. Anyway, my brother, he broke it while

speaker: ES
words: I was in the Service.

speaker: SS
words: At least, it stayed in the family.

speaker: ES
words: Yeah, uh-huh. In those days, you did pretty good if you run a mile in

speaker: ES
words: five minutes, that was pretty good. I think the record I set in high

speaker: ES
words: school was 5W, I ran it a little better than that at one time, but I

speaker: ES
words: wasn't in competition. I was running with the—the kids was running the

speaker: ES
words: "B80 the half mile and they run me in the 680 with them, and then

speaker: ES
words: they ran with me in the mile. That's when I had my best record, but

speaker: ES
words: 'course that wasn't competition then. It was 5' something. But the

speaker: ES
words: record I set was 5".Course they run the mile now — they run it in

speaker: ES
words: 31- something now!I (Chuckles) So you see, it wasn't very fast!! Had

speaker: ES
words: lots of fun though.

speaker: SS
words: Did you do a lot of ?

speaker: ES
words: In the wintertime, we started early in the spring— you see we didn't

speaker: ES
words: have no regular coach. They didn't hire a high school football or track

speaker: ES
words: coach— they got some junior or senior at the University to work us out.

speaker: ES
words: The athletic program wasn't much. They didn't^have their own coach,

speaker: ES
words: they just got a kid from the University, junior or senior at the Univsity

speaker: ES
words: to handle it. And they fixed it so in the ealjry spring, why we'd

speaker: ES
words: go up to the University on certain evenings and use the girls' gym up

speaker: ES
words: there for our training indoors, in the wintertime. And we did our other

speaker: ES
words: training up on the University field. Of course, we played our football

speaker: ES
words: down here at6a£Hiley Park, down on West Sixth Street there, that's where

speaker: ES
words: we played football, down there.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that sport was as important to the school then as it is

speaker: SS
words: now?

speaker: ES
words: Well, as I say, when I started there, it wasn't very important. But,

speaker: ES
words: of course, they built up from there, and it is very important at the

speaker: ES
words: present time. But it didn't amount to much when I started to high school.

speaker: ES
words: Because all the kids went to the University up there. There was nobody

speaker: ES
words: they built the high school

speaker: ES
words: there to—until from 1912 tney started building up and by the time

speaker: ES
words: my nephew got in high school, why it was a big deal then. And then we

speaker: ES
words: played— well, we played schools like Colfax and all these little one

speaker: ES
words: horse towns, Genesee. 'Course, now, Moscow it plays *n a different

speaker: ES
words: league altogether, they don't play nobody smaller than Lewiston. They

speaker: ES
words: play Lewiston and the Spokane High Schools and Coeur d'Alene and places

speaker: ES
words: like that, but when I went to high school, we played Oakesdale, all

speaker: ES
words: these little town around here, Colfax and Troy, wherever they had a high

speaker: ES
words: school.

speaker: SS
words: Seems like now-a-days they're big community events, too.

speaker: ES
words: Yes, you bet they are. You bet they're big community events.

speaker: SS
words: I wonder, did you have a lot of fans come along to root in those days?

speaker: ES
words: No, in those days, it wasn't nothing more than the student bodies. Oh,

speaker: ES
words: unless we had kind of a interstate track meet. Now, we had the District

speaker: ES
words: Track Meet— like we had at Lewiston. The first time I was ever in

speaker: ES
words: Lewiston, I was down there for a track meet, that was in 19— the spring

speaker: ES
words: of 1913. The Lewiston track meet. They had schools there from Boise —

speaker: ES
words: That was a big event down there then. And I ran with a kid down there

speaker: ES
words: on the Lewiston Normal Lewiston Normal is the State College now— he

speaker: ES
words: was from Grangeville in 1913, and in 1919— and then on May the 30th

speaker: ES
words: 1919 I ran with him in Paris. We ran a relay race from Chateau-Thierry

speaker: ES
words: to Paris, that's fifty miles. And we had about twenty-five guys on each

speaker: ES
words: team, and the team I was on, I ran the last two miles, and my last two

speaker: ES
words: miles took me right in the heart of Paris, because we finished at the

speaker: ES
words: Arch of Triumph,in Paris. And I ran the last two miles right down the

speaker: ES
words: streets in Paris. The main streets of Paris.This kid was with me.

speaker: SS
words: You say that's the first time you'd ever been to Lewiston?

speaker: ES
words: Yeah, 1913.

speaker: SS
words: I guess kids didn't travel around very much in those days.

speaker: SS
words: those days,remember.

speaker: ES
words: Well, in those days there wasn't any cars, You went horseback or you

speaker: ES
words: went in a buggy, drove a team. My second automfiiflle ride, was I came

speaker: ES
words: from Lewiston, from that track meet, I rode back to Moscow with a kid—

speaker: ES
words: his dad was — worked for an implement company down here in Moscow, set-up

speaker: ES
words: set-Machinery, and he had an old Model T Ford that he went around to

speaker: ES
words: farm machinery with. And he came down to the track meet in that

speaker: ES
words: old Ford Roadster and his son and I rode back with him. That was my

speaker: ES
words: second ride in an automobile. And my folks got their first automobile

speaker: ES
words: in the fall of 1919 after I got back from WWI That's when I learned

speaker: ES
words: to drive a car in 1919. They bought a car.

speaker: SS
words: Do you remember that second ride you took? Was that up the Lewiston

speaker: SS
words: Hill?

speaker: SS
words: The Lewiston Hill, uh-huh.

speaker: SS
words: I imagine that must have been thrilling.

speaker: ES
words: I'll say so. Well, you know, going down the hill where that spring is

speaker: ES
words: down along— there used to be a little store sit there. And in the early

speaker: ES
words: days, the first of the Model T Fords, they used to have to get up that

speaker: ES
